_description: Parts catalog for the Claudia build. Each part has a 'searchFor' Google Shopping URL plus at least 3 direct tier links (amazon -> official -> reputable). The 'find-deals' console command opens these in order so you can pick the best price; 'apply-deals' stamps the chosen URL back into Claudia.md by replacing 'match' with a Markdown link. Prices are NON-AUTHORITATIVE estimates - they go stale fast. Refresh by querying current Google Shopping results (per part) and updating both 'price' (USD, integer dollars) and 'pricesAsOf' (date last verified, top-level).
pricesNote: Approximate USD retail at the date above. Click any part card to verify against current Google Shopping results.

categories.portable: Optional battery for a roaming build.
categories.smarthome: Optional locally-controllable smart plugs. Lets Claudia run commands like 'Claudia turn on the living room' without any cloud round-trip beyond Claude itself.

parts.name: Raspberry Pi Zero 2 WH
parts.note: Must be the WH (with pre-soldered headers). The plain W has no GPIO pins and would need 40 pins soldered before the WonderEcho's 4-pin cable can connect. Most retailers list WH as a dropdown variant of the W product page — pick 'with headers' before adding to cart. Supply is intermittent — if all US retailers show out-of-stock, rpilocator.com tracks live availability.

specs.label: SKU
specs.value: SC0511 (RPi Foundation) · Adafruit 6008 · SparkFun DEV-26256

specs.label: CPU
specs.value: Quad-core Arm Cortex-A53 @ 1 GHz, 64-bit ARMv8

specs.label: SoC
specs.value: Raspberry Pi RP3A0 SiP (Broadcom BCM2710A1 die)

specs.label: RAM
specs.value: 512 MB LPDDR2

specs.label: Wireless
specs.value: Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n 2.4 GHz · Bluetooth 4.2 / BLE

specs.label: GPIO
specs.value: 40-pin pre-soldered header (the “H” suffix)

specs.label: Power in
specs.value: 5 V via micro-USB (the corner port labeled “PWR IN”)

parts.name: microSD card, 32 GB Class 10 (SanDisk Ultra)
parts.match: **microSD card, 32 GB Class 10** (SanDisk Ultra or equivalent)
parts.note: Ships with a full-size SD-card adapter only — no USB reader. If your laptop has no SD slot (most modern ultrabooks/MacBooks don't), grab a USB microSD reader too (~$8).

specs.label: SKU
specs.value: SDSQUA4-032G-AN6MA (SanDisk Ultra microSDHC 32 GB)

specs.label: Capacity
specs.value: 32 GB

specs.label: Speed
specs.value: Class 10, UHS-I U1, A1 app-rated · up to 120 MB/s read

specs.label: Used for
specs.value: Raspberry Pi OS 64-bit (full image) — flashed in Part 04 via Raspberry Pi Imager

specs.label: In the box
specs.value: microSD card + full-size SD adapter. No USB reader included.

specs.label: Substitutes
specs.value: Any reputable 32 GB Class 10 / A1 microSD works (Samsung EVO Plus, Kingston Canvas Select, etc.)

parts.name: Official Raspberry Pi 12.5W micro-USB power supply (5V/2.5A)
parts.note: The Pi Zero 2 WH is micro-USB, NOT USB-C. A USB-C-only PSU will not fit. Plugs into the corner port labeled “PWR IN” on the silkscreen, not the middle “USB” port. Any clean 5 V / 2.5 A micro-USB supply works — the official one is recommended because it has a thick captive cable and won't brown out under load.

specs.label: Output
specs.value: 5.1 V / 2.5 A DC (12.5 W nominal, 12.75 W actual)

specs.label: Connector
specs.value: micro-USB-B (captive) — NOT USB-C

specs.label: Cable
specs.value: 1.5 m, 18 AWG

specs.label: Plugs into
specs.value: Pi Zero 2 WH “PWR IN” port (corner micro-USB, not the middle “USB” port)

specs.label: Regions
specs.value: US / UK / EU / AU / IN plug variants — pick at retailer checkout

parts.name: PiSugar 3 1200 mAh battery
parts.note: Pick the “PiSugar 3” for the Pi Zero form factor — NOT the larger “PiSugar 3 Plus” (which is sized for the full Pi 4). The 1200 mAh variant is the standard one.

specs.label: Capacity
specs.value: 1200 mAh Li-Po

specs.label: Fits
specs.value: Raspberry Pi Zero / Zero 2 W (NOT the larger “Plus” for Pi 4)

specs.label: Mount
specs.value: Magnetic pogo pins — no soldering, snaps to the underside of the Pi

specs.label: Extras
specs.value: On-board RTC, USB-C charge port, power button, IRQ button

specs.label: Software
specs.value: PiSugar Power Manager (Python service) — optional for OS-level battery awareness

parts.name: TP-Link Kasa HS103 / KP125M smart plug (local control via python-kasa)
parts.note: Works locally over the LAN with the python-kasa library — no cloud round-trip. Tell Claudia 'turn on the living room' and a one-line shell call flips it.

specs.label: Model
specs.value: HS103 (single) / HS103P2 (2-pack). KP125M is the newer Matter-capable equivalent and also works with python-kasa.

specs.label: Max load
specs.value: 10 A resistive @ 120 VAC (1200 W typical, general-use rating)

specs.label: Wi-Fi
specs.value: 2.4 GHz only · 802.11 b/g/n (Pi Zero 2 W also 2.4 GHz only)

specs.label: Form
specs.value: “Mini” single outlet, 66.5 × 40 × 38 mm

specs.label: Local API
specs.value: TP-Link Smart Home LAN protocol — “kasa --host <ip> on/off” via python-kasa, no Kasa cloud account required

parts.name: Shelly Plug US (local HTTP / MQTT, no cloud required)
parts.note: REST endpoint at http://<ip>/relay/0?turn=on — Claudia can flip it with a single curl. No vendor account needed. Current model is Shelly Plug US Gen4; the legacy /relay/ endpoint still works alongside the new /rpc/ JSON-RPC.

specs.label: Generation
specs.value: Shelly Plug US Gen4 (current model) — exposes the new /rpc/ JSON-RPC and the legacy /relay/ endpoints

specs.label: Max load
specs.value: 12 A continuous / 15 A peak (1440 W @ 120 VAC)

specs.label: Local API
specs.value: HTTP REST + MQTT + WebSocket. Guide uses GET /relay/0?turn=on|off (works on every Shelly generation).

specs.label: Extras
specs.value: On-board energy metering · no vendor account needed for local control

parts.name: Hiwonder WonderEcho voice module (I2C wake-word frontend)
parts.note: Ships with the 4-pin Dupont cable used to connect to the Pi's GPIO header — no extra wires to buy. Verify the box contains 1× WonderEcho + 1× 4-pin cable before assembling.

specs.label: SKU
specs.value: Hiwonder 21090150 · Amazon ASIN B0F7RR983M

specs.label: In the box
specs.value: 1× WonderEcho module + 1× 4-pin Dupont cable (per Hiwonder packing list)

specs.label: Voice chip
specs.value: CI1302 (on-device CNN wake-word + recognition firmware)

specs.label: Interface
specs.value: I²C bus 1, default address 0x52 — verify with “i2cdetect -y 1”

specs.label: Connector
specs.value: 4-pin cable: SDA → BCM 2 (pin 3), SCL → BCM 3 (pin 5), 5V → pin 2, GND → pin 6

specs.label: On-board
specs.value: Microphone (noise-reducing) + speaker + red/blue status LEDs

specs.label: Memory
specs.value: 2 MB Flash, 640 KB SRAM

specs.label: Range
specs.value: ~5 m quiet / ~1 m noisy · up to 255 phrases

specs.label: Languages
specs.value: English + Chinese

parts.name: Sonoff S31 (re-flashable with Tasmota for local MQTT control)
parts.note: Out-of-the-box uses the eWeLink cloud; flash with Tasmota to get fully local MQTT/HTTP control. Pick the S31 (with energy monitoring) — NOT the S31 Lite, which omits the power meter.

specs.label: Model
specs.value: S31 (with power monitoring) — NOT S31 Lite

specs.label: MCU
specs.value: Espressif ESP8266

specs.label: Energy chip
specs.value: CSE7766 (UART-attached power meter on GPIO01/03)

specs.label: Stock firmware
specs.value: eWeLink (cloud-dependent — does NOT meet the “local only” goal as-shipped)

specs.label: Re-flashable
specs.value: Yes — Tasmota via serial header inside the case (no soldering needed for the S31)

specs.label: Reference
specs.value: Tasmota template + flash steps: templates.blakadder.com/sonoff_S31.html
